'My bottom raven black-one moment madam!- sensing
Her receiver rearing on the thunderclap'

The extract above from Wole Soyinka's "Telephone Conversation" shows the friction between
solution: This extract in Wole Soyinka's "Telephone Conversation," illustrates the tension and friction between the landlady and the tenant. The landlady's response to the tenant's race, as he describes it, creates an awkward and charged atmosphere. The lines convey a sense of confusion, tension and awkwardness that arises when the landlady reacts to the tenant’s race without understanding or respect.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from:

R. Johnson and D. Ker er al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Wole Soyinka (ed.): Poems of Black Africa; K.E. Senanu and T. Vincent (eds.): A Selection of African Poetry; M. Umukoro and A. Sani et al (eds.) Exam Focus: Literature in English; A.E. Eruvbetine and M. Jibril et al (eds.): Longman Examination Guides: Poetry and E.W. Parker (ed.): A Pageant of Longer Poems.

In Wole Soyinka's "Telephone Conversation," the dominant literary technique deployed is
solution: In Wole Soyinka's Telephone Conversation, the dominant literary technique used is alliteration. Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in a poem. Soyinka makes use of alliteration in the poem to make it more rhythmic and memorable.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from:

R. Johnson and D. Ker er al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Wole Soyinka (ed.): Poems of Black Africa; K.E. Senanu and T. Vincent (eds.): A Selection of African Poetry; M. Umukoro and A. Sani et al (eds.) Exam Focus: Literature in English; A.E. Eruvbetine and M. Jibril et al (eds.): Longman Examination Guides: Poetry and E.W. Parker (ed.): A Pageant of Longer Poems.

David Rubadiri's "Stanley meets Mutesa" symbolically describes the meeting between
solution: In Stanley Meets Mutesa, David Rubadiri uses the meeting between the European explorer, Henry Morton Stanley, and the African king, Mutesa, as a symbol of the encounter between the West (represented by Stanley) and Africa (represented by Mutesa). This symbolic meeting represents the colonial encounter, where Western powers, through exploration and imperialism, imposed their presence and influence on African societies.

options.a: a black king and a white man

question:
This question is based on selected poems from:

R. Johnson and D. Ker er al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Wole Soyinka (ed.): Poems of Black Africa; K.E. Senanu and T. Vincent (eds.): A Selection of African Poetry; M. Umukoro and A. Sani et al (eds.) Exam Focus: Literature in English; A.E. Eruvbetine and M. Jibril et al (eds.): Longman Examination Guides: Poetry and E.W. Parker (ed.): A Pageant of Longer Poems.

The pervasive mood of the speaker in Mtshali's 'Nightfall in Soweto' is that of
solution: In Nightfall in Soweto by Mtshali, the mood is one of terror and insecurity. The speaker describes the unsettling atmosphere of nightfall in Soweto, where the darkness evokes feelings of fear and vulnerability due to the social and political conditions of apartheid South Africa. The oppressive environment of the area is depicted through the imagery of darkness and the threats posed by the night.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from:

R. Johnson and D. Ker er al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Wole Soyinka (ed.): Poems of Black Africa; K.E. Senanu and T. Vincent (eds.): A Selection of African Poetry; M. Umukoro and A. Sani et al (eds.) Exam Focus: Literature in English; A.E. Eruvbetine and M. Jibril et al (eds.): Longman Examination Guides: Poetry and E.W. Parker (ed.): A Pageant of Longer Poems.

The stanza form of Mapanje's "When this Carnival Finally Closes" is
solution: In "When This Carnival Finally Closes" by David Mapanje, the poem is written in free verse, meaning it does not follow a specific rhyme scheme or meter. Free verse allows for more fluid expression and is often used to reflect the themes of liberation and the irregularity of life's experiences, which is characteristic of Mapanje's poetry.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from Ker, D. et al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Soyinka, W. (ed.): Poems of Black African; Senanu, K.E. and Vincent, T.(ds.): A Selection of African Poetry; Umukoro, M. et al (eds.); Exam Focus: Literature-in-English; Eruvbetine, A.E. et al (eds.) Longman Examination Guides and Nwoga, D.I. (ed.):West African Verse

The dominant images in Niyi Osundare's "They Too are the Earth" are
solution: In Niyi Osundare's poem "They Too Are the Earth", the dominant images are poverty, suffering and the downtrodden. The poem portrays the struggles of the maginalised, their suffering and their rightful place in the world. Osundare speaks out for those who are oppressed, portraying them as being integral to the earth itself, despite their hardships.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from: R. Johnson and D. Ker er al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Wole Soyinka (ed.): Poems of Black Africa; K.E. Senanu and T. Vincent (eds.): A Selection of African Poetry; M. Umukoro and A. Sani et al (eds.) Exam Focus: Literature in English; A.E. Eruvbetine and M. Jibril et al (eds.): Longman Examination Guides: Poetry and E.W. Parker (ed.): A Pageant of Longer Poems.

'... It is not yet dawn
And we wake from one nightmare
To another.'

In these lines from Ojaide's "The Owl Wake Us", the mood is that of a
solution: In the lines "It is not yet dawn / And we wake from one nightmare / To another," the mood conveyed is one of ongoing hardship and suffering. The speaker reflects a sense of unrelenting distress, where one troubling situation gives way to another, without any respite. This portrays a vicious cycle of oppression, where they cannot escape from their struggles.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from Ker, D. et al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Soyinka, W. (ed.): Poems of Black African; Senanu, K.E. and Vincent, T.(ds.): A Selection of African Poetry; Umukoro, M. et al (eds.); Exam Focus: Literature-in-English; Eruvbetine, A.E. et al (eds.) Longman Examination Guides and Nwoga, D.I. (ed.):West African Verse

In Browning's "My Last Duchess'" the poet persona's attitude toward the Duchess is that of
solution: In "My Last Duchess", the speaker, the Duke of Ferrara, reveals his deep scorn for his late wife, the Duchess. Throughout the poem, the Duke speaks of her in a detached and cold manner and criticises her for being too kind, too flirtatious and not showing him the proper respect. His attitude is not one of love or sadness, but rather disdain and possessiveness as he discusses how he had her silenced (presumably through her death) because she did not live up to his expectations.

question:
This question is based on selected poems from Ker, D. et al (eds.): New Poetry from Africa; Soyinka, W. (ed.): Poems of Black African; Senanu, K.E. and Vincent, T.(ds.): A Selection of African Poetry; Umukoro, M. et al (eds.); Exam Focus: Literature-in-English; Eruvbetine, A.E. et al (eds.) Longman Examination Guides and Nwoga, D.I. (ed.):West African Verse

In "Ode on a Grecian Urn," John Keats is concerned with
solution: In "Ode on a Grecian Urn," John Keats reflects on the nature of art and its ability to preserve beauty and emotions across time. The poem contrasts the fleeting nature of human life with the timeless, unchanging beauty captured in the scenes depicted on the urn.

options.a: the impermanence of the Grecian Urn

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The term for the moral flaw or weakness that leads to the downfall of a major character in drama is
solution: Hamartia is the tragic flaw or moral weakness in the hero of a tragic work that leads to their downfall or misfortune in a tragedy. It is a key concept in classical drama, especially in Greek tragedies.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The figure of speech in which a character makes a statement that has or would have deep and serious implications in the play is
solution: Euphemism is a figure of speech that helps to soften the blow or impact of an otherwise deep and serious subject matter e.g "He kicked the bucket" is a euphemism for he died. When a character makes a statement in a mild way about something that is deep or has serious implication, for the event that unfolds in the play, euphemism has been used.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A character who does not develop or experience change in the course of his or her existence in a novel is a
solution: A flat character is one who does not undergo significant development or change throughout the story. They have one or two traits and are not complex or fully developed. In contrast to round characters, flat characters tend to remain static and predictable.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A panegyric poem is composed to
solution: A panegyric is a type of poem or speech that is composed to praise someone or something, often in an elaborate or formal way. Panegyric poems are written to celebrate the virtues, achievements or qualities of a person, a group or even an idea.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The narrator in a prose work who is also a character is
solution: A participatory narrator is one who is also a character within the story. This narrator not only tells the story but also plays a role in the events. This is commonly seen in first-person narratives where the narrator uses "I" and is involved in the actions of the story.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A primary ballad is associated with
solution: A primary ballad is a traditional form of folk song that originated in rural communities. It uses simple, direct language and tells a story about everyday life, love or historical events. These ballads were passed down orally and were usually sung by rural folk.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A trilogy is the
solution: This is the correct definition of a trilogy. A trilogy is typically a series of three works that are related in theme, plot or characters and are often divided into three parts with each part contributing to the overarching story.

options.a: sequence of three one-act drama written by the same author
options.b: set of three plays written by the same authors
options.c: collection of three poems of equal length
options.d: series of related stories divided into three equal parts

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

A novel which focuses on the adventures of a rogue who does not change much in the course of the story is
solution: The picaresque novel focuses on the adventures of a picaro—a roguish, often lower-class protagonist—who embarks on a series of adventures. The key feature of a picaresque novel is that the protagonist does not undergo significant character development or change throughout the story.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

The term given to a type of incident or device which recurs frequently in Literature is
solution: A motif is a recurring element, theme or incident in a literary work, such as a repeated image, idea or symbol. It often plays a significant role in developing the theme or message of the work.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Using the name of one thing for something else with which it is closely associated is an instance of
solution: Metonymy is a literary device where one thing is used to refer to something closely associated with it. For example, using "the crown" to refer to the monarchy or "the White House" to refer to the President or the U.S. government.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Mock-heroic poetry elevates
solution: Mock-heroic poetry is a satirical form of poetry that imitates the grand style of classical epic poetry (such as Homer's Iliad or Odyssey), but it applies this elevated style to trivial or mundane subjects. The contrast between the high style and low subject matter creates humour or irony.

options.a: the beauty in human relationships as exemplified in Homer
options.b: trivial subject-matter by using the style of the classical epic
options.c: the stripping off of appearances in a witty manner
options.d: the important tales of heroes of the past era

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles.

Lineation refers to
solution: Lineation is the way in which lines of poetry are arranged or structured in a poem. It deals with how the lines are broken up, the use of stanzas and how the poet decides to organize the text visually and rhythmically.

options.a: tracing family descent of poeple in verse
options.b: the unit in the rhythmic structure of verse
options.c: the arrangement of lines in verse form
options.d: the grouping together of a number of units of rhythm

question:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'He would hear the heavy uneven breathing of the child. It was as if she were carrying a weight with great effort up a long hill...He prayed again ''Father, look after her. Give her peace...Take away my peace forever, but give her peace''

Graham Greene, The Heart of the Matter.

The man's reactions to the presence of the dying child show that he is
solution: The man’s reactions—his awareness of the child's suffering ("carrying a weight with great effort up a long hill") and his heartfelt prayer ("Take away my peace forever, but give her peace")—demonstrate deep empathy and concern for the child’s well-being. This selflessness and desire to ease her pain reflect compassion.

question:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'In Umuaro it is not our custom to refuse a call, although we may refuse to do what the caller asks.  Ezeulu does not want to refuse the white man's call and so he is sending his son!'

Chinua Achebe, Arrow of God

The lines above illustrate the use of
solution: In the lines provided, Achebe uses a statement about custom—"In Umuaro it is not our custom to refuse a call, although we may refuse to do what the caller asks"—to convey a deeper meaning. This is the essence of a proverb; it expresses wisdom or cultural norms. The proverb is extended through the reference to Ezeulu's actions, showing how he adheres to the custom but also manipulates it to avoid confrontation. Traditional parables are longer stories that teach a moral lesson.

question:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'Those who have nothing but guns for the hungry and think of nothing but death and dying let them spend our earth's fortune harvesting blood from the fields of war. The last banquet shall be their children's blood.

Kofi Anyidoho, "Blood Harvest"

The stanza above succinctly presents the
solution: The stanza strongly critiques war and violence. It portrays the destruction and loss of life that it brings. The line "harvesting blood from the fields of war" metaphorically represents the brutal and bloody consequences of war and violence. It also shows the devastating impact of war on future generations, as shown by the reference to "the last banquet shall be their children's blood." This highlights the central issue of war and violence and its destructive consequences.

question:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

'Thus; quixoting till a cast-off of my land I sing and fare, person to loved-one pressed braced for this pressure and the captor's hand that snaps off service like a weathered strand...'

Dennis Brutus 'A Troubadour I Traverse'

In the lines above, the poet-persona expresses
solution: The lines suggest a stance of resistance or struggle. The phrase "quixoting till a cast-off of my land" evokes an image of someone persistently fighting for a cause, even if it seems futile, similar to Don Quixote's battle against imaginary foes. The "captor's hand that snaps off service like a weathered strand" further suggests a refusal to yield or submit easily. This portrays defiance in the face of oppression.

question:
This question is based on Literary Appreciation.

Africa my Africa of proud warriors in ancestral savannahs...
I have never known you but your blood flows in my veins
Your beautiful black blood that irrigates the fields....

David Diop, "Africa"

In the lines above, Diop uses
solution: A metaphor is a figure of speech that directly compares two things without using "like" or "as." In the lines "Your beautiful black blood that irrigates the fields," the blood of Africa is metaphorically compared to something that nourishes or brings life, much like water irrigates fields. The comparison between blood and irrigation is symbolic of the life force and vitality of Africa, making this a metaphor.

Which of these features give the novel an African setting ?

This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's Second Class Citizen.
In Adah's view, the white society in the novel is one in which
solution:
In Second Class Citizen by Buchi Emecheta, Adah, the protagonist, experiences the harsh realities of being an immigrant in Britain. She finds that the white society is largely individualistic, and there is a lack of warmth and communal support compared to the close-knit community she was used to in Nigeria. This shows that the members of the community are unfriendly towards immigrants.

In Buchi Emecheta's Second Class Citizen, Adah’s experience with the white society reflects a lack of neighbourliness and a sense of isolation. Several key moments in the novel support this view:

  1. Adah's Isolation in the Neighborhood:
    When Adah and her family first move to England, they are faced with a society that is indifferent and often hostile to them. Their neighbours show little interest in befriending them. Adah notes the lack of warmth or communal bonds in the area. This is reflected in her initial struggles to connect with those around her. The coldness of her neighbours leaves her feeling isolated, even though she is physically surrounded by people.

  2. Racial Prejudice and Segregation:
    Throughout the novel, Adah experiences racism and prejudice, which further underscores the lack of neighbourliness. Her efforts to engage with the white society are often met with rejection or indifference. This is reflected in the difficulty she had while searching for a new apartment in London as everywhere she went, the sign that read "Sorry, No Coloureds" was posted.  It is also reflected in the fact that Trudy was able to get away with the negligence of the children placed under her care, even when there was glaring evidence that she was a bad caretaker.

These examples illustrate that Adah’s view of white society in Second Class Citizen is one in which neighbourliness is indeed lacking, as racial prejudice and indifference create barriers to connection and communal feeling.

A melodramatic play is characterized by:

shaven clean as a potato
whispered to the rising sun,
a red eye wiped by a tattered
handkerchief of clouds.'
Mtshali, 'Men in Chains
The figures of speech employed in the lines above are
solution:
• Simile – A simile is a comparison using "like" or "as."

    • In the line "shaven clean as a potato," the man's bald head is being compared to a potato using "as," making this a simile.

  • Metaphor – A metaphor is a direct comparison between two unrelated things without using "like" or "as."

    • In "the rising sun, a red eye wiped by a tattered handkerchief of clouds," the sun is metaphorically described as a red eye, and the clouds are metaphorically compared to a tattered handkerchief, making this a metaphor.

This question is based on General Literary Principles.
Which of the following are literary genres?
solution:
The correct answer is B. Fiction, poetry, and drama are literary genres
- Fiction: This includes novels, short stories, and other narratives that are created from the imagination.
- Poetry: This includes lyrical and narrative poems, where language is used for its aesthetic and evocative qualities.
- Drama: This includes plays, scripts, and performances designed for the stage, often involving dialogue and acting.

The other options don't include a combination of recognized literary genres.

question:

What is an epic?
solution: An epic is a long narrative poem that celebrates the heroic deeds and achievements of a legendary or historical figure. It often includes supernatural elements, grand adventures, and reflects the values of the culture it represents. Examples include Homer's Iliad and Odyssey and Milton's Paradise Lost.

The Christs
are still in performing miracles
in the market places
heroding the masses with imperatives
feeding the hungry
with 21-gun salutes
for victories that are yet to be won.'
Odia Ofeimun, 'The Messiahs'
The attitude of the poet to his subject in the lines above is
solution: Sarcasm is a form of verbal irony that involves using language to convey the opposite of what is actually meant, often with a mocking or scornful tone. In the poem, phrases like "heroding the masses with imperatives" suggest that the leaders are acting like King Herod, known for his authoritarian and oppressive rule, thus ridiculing their style of governance. Similarly, "feeding the hungry with 21-gun salutes" sarcastically implies that the leaders are celebrating symbolic victories or gestures instead of addressing real issues such as hunger and poverty.

John Keats, 'Ode to a Nightingale'. The above lines are an example of
solution:
Consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds, especially at the end or within words.
– Look at “beaded bubbles winking at the brim” – we see repeated /b/ sounds.
– “Purple-stained mouth”- repeated /m/ sounds.

question:
This question is based on Literary Principles.
'Still stand stubborn
To stones that strangle the dawn,
Still stand stubborn
To stones that maim the morn,
still stand stubborn
To stones that assail the sun'
Atukwei Okai, 'Sunset Sonata'
The dominant literary device employed in the above passage is
solution:
Alliteration is a literary device where a series of words in a sentence or phrase share the same initial consonant sound. In the provided passage, the repetition of the initial "s" sound in the phrase "Still stand stubborn" and throughout the lines is an example of alliteration. This repetitive use of the same consonant sound creates a musical and rhythmic quality. It draws attention to the words and emphasises the defiance and resilience expressed in the poem.

While repetition is present in the passage, the more prominent and distinctive feature is the alliteration. The title of the poem, "Sunset Sonata," also exhibits alliteration with the repeated "s" sound and this reinforces the stylistic choice of the poet.

solution:
Kofi Awoonor’s poem “Songs of Sorrow” reflects on the inevitability and finality of death, a common theme in African poetry addressing personal loss, ancestral memory, and grief.

In the poem:

  • The mourning and lamentation emphasise that once someone dies, they cannot return to life.

  • The tone is one of acceptance of loss, rather than suggesting continuity or temporary death.

Thus, the central idea is that death ends the earthly existence of an individual — it is final.

options.c: the living communicate with the dead

When a story is told in a song and passed down by word of mouth, it is called
solution: When a story is told in a song and passed down by word of mouth, it is called a ballad. A ballad is a narrative poem or song that tells a story, often of a romantic or sentimental nature, and is passed down orally from one generation to the next. It is usually composed of quatrains (four-line stanzas) with a rhyme scheme of ABAB or ABCB. Ballads are often accompanied by music and are a popular form of folk poetry.

This question is based on General Literary Principles.
Comic relief is
solution:
Comic relief refers to the inclusion of humorous elements, characters, or scenes within a primarily serious or tragic work, with the intention of providing relief to the audience from the emotional intensity or tension of the main plot. The purpose of comic relief is to offer moments of laughter or light-heartedness amidst the more somber or intense themes of a tragedy or dramatic work.

So, option A accurately describes comic relief as the use of humorous elements within a tragic work to provide relief from the seriousness of the main plot.

The two characters that symbolize the lion and the jewel respectively are
solution:
In Wole Soyinka’s The Lion and the Jewel:

  • Baroka, the Bale of Ilujinle, symbolises the Lion.
    He represents strength, power, authority, cunning and traditional dominance.

  • Sidi, the village beauty, symbolises the Jewel.
    She represents beauty, pride, youth and the object of desire around which the conflict of the play revolves.

question:

'This is the words of women. At this moment our star sits in the center of the sky
We are supreme.'
These words were spoken by

This question is based on Wole Soyinka's The Lion and the Jewel.
The Bale is symbolically referred to as a fox because of his
solution:
In Wole Soyinka’s The Lion and the Jewel,  Lakunle, the schoolteacher, refers to Baroka, the Bale (traditional village chief), as a fox. This metaphor symbolises Baroka’s cunning, cleverness and strategic thinking.

This happened when Sidi, Sadiku and Lakunle were having a conversation. Sidi tells them that she is familiar with Baroka's "little supper" that he uses to lure unsuspecting maidens to his bed. It is then that Lakunle says: "Is it for nothing he is called the Fox?" (p.23). Lakunle narrates how Baroka had bribed a white surveyor not to allow the railway to pass through the village so that he could continue to maintain his power over his people. This suggests that the label Fox symbolises Baroka's mental prowess. 

The fox is a classic symbol of craftiness and mental agility, not necessarily physical strength or sexual ability, although Baroka does display some of those traits. However, in this context, the emphasis is on his ability to manipulate and outwit others.

question:

In what type of play would the playwright introduce a comic relief to relieve tension already built up?
solution: In a tragedy, where the plot often involves serious and intense events leading to a downfall or unfortunate outcome for the protagonist, playwrights may use comic relief to provide moments of humour or light-heartedness. This contrast can help to alleviate the tension and create a more dynamic and engaging theatrical experience for the audience.

Sitting on my head
The World
I wear the Centaur's hide
And I have carried the World since the first morning'.
'I Thank You God' by Benard Dadie
The poet's attitude here is being
solution: The poet's attitude in this excerpt from "I Thank You God" by Bernard Dadie is ironical because there is a contradiction between the speaker's gratitude and the heavy burdens he mentions. While the speaker is thanking God for creating him black and for giving him the burden of carrying the sorrows of the world, there is an underlying sense of irony.

solution: A foil is a character in a literary work who contrasts with another character (usually the protagonist) to highlight particular qualities of that character. This contrast makes the traits of the main character more prominent.

This question is based on General Literature Principles and Literary Appreciation.
'I die, yet depart not,
I am bound, yet soar free;
Thou art and thou art not,
And ever shall be!'
'The City of Dreams' by Robert Buchanan. The literary device consciously used in the above extract is
solution:
A paradox is a statement that appears self-contradictory or illogical but may reveal a deeper truth upon reflection. In the lines from The City of Dreams by Robert Buchanan:

"I die, yet depart not,"

"I am bound, yet soar free;"

"Thou art and thou art not,"

Each statement presents a contradiction. The speaker claims to simultaneously experience opposing states—dying but not departing, being bound yet free, and existing while not existing. These contradictions challenge conventional logic, making the passage a clear example of a paradox.

solution: A ballad is a short narrative song or poem that tells a story about adventure, love or tragedy. It combines both music (song) and narrative (storytelling) elements. This distinguishes it from just a short literary song (A) or a short story (B). Option C (“short story that contains a song”) is not accurate because a ballad itself is a song telling a story , not a story embedding a song.

"Here lies our sovereign Lord the King,
Whose word no man relies on,
Who never said a foolish thing
Nor ever did a wise one,"

Based on John Wilmot Rochester's Epitaph on King Charles 11, the form of the above stanza is an example of
solution:
An epigram is a brief, witty, and often paradoxical statement or poem that expresses an idea in a clever or satirical way.

 

In this stanza, John Wilmot, Earl of Rochester, makes a sharp and ironic comment about King Charles II. The paradox in the last two lines—"Who never said a foolish thing / Nor ever did a wise one"—is a classic example of an epigrammatic style, as it presents a witty contradiction.

question:

solution:
An allegory is a work of art, such as a story or painting, in which the characters, images, and/or events act as symbols. The symbolism in an allegory can be interpreted to have a deeper meaning. An author may use allegory to illustrate a moral or spiritual truth, or political or historical situation.
Allegory is an attempt through which abstract or symbol is used to portray evils and ills common in our society.

question: The role of the Storyteller in The Marriage of Anansewa is that of a
solution:
In The Marriage of Anansewa by Efua Sutherland, the Storyteller functions mainly as a commentator.

He:

  • Narrates events to the audience

  • Explains cultural practices

  • Provides background information

  • Guides the audience’s understanding of actions

  • Bridges the gap between traditional oral storytelling and modern drama

This reflects the influence of African oral tradition, where the storyteller interprets events and engages the audience directly.

This question is based on Wole Soyinka's The Lion and the Jewel.


The time has come when I can fool myself no more I am no man Sadiku My manhood Ended near a week ago'.

In the light of subsequent happenings, these word reveal that the speaker
solution:
The speaker of these lines is Baroka, the Bale (chief) of Ilujinle, in The Lion and the Jewel by Wole Soyinka. He tells Sadiku, his senior wife, that he has become impotent. However, this is later revealed to be a trick to manipulate Sidi, the village belle, into marrying him.

By spreading the false news of his impotence through Sadiku, Baroka plays on Sidi’s emotions, making her feel confident and in control. However, he eventually seduces her, proving that his claim is a deceptive strategy rather than the truth. This shows Baroka's cunning nature and ability to manipulate situations to his advantage, making option C (a crafty seducer) the best choice.

question:
This question is based on General Literary Principles and Appreciation.

'If it is true that the world talks too much
then let's all keep quiet
and hear the eloquence
of silence'
If it is True' by Richard Ntini
One striking device used by the poet above is
solution:
In Richard Ntini's poem "If it is True", the poet contrasts "talking too much" with the idea of "keeping quiet" to highlight the power and meaning of silence. This use of opposing ideas is a clear example of contrast, a literary device that shows the difference between two concepts to emphasise one or both.

Line-by-line Analysis:

"If it is true that the world talks too much
then let's all keep quiet*
and hear the eloquence*
of silence"*

  • The world’s noise (talking too much) is set against the quiet (keeping silent).

  • The unexpected twist is calling silence "eloquent", which intensifies the contrast—silence vs. speech.

question: In Oswald Mtshali's 'Nightfall in Soweto', night
solution: In the poem, the night is portrayed as a time when the dangers and challenges faced by the people of Soweto are concealed or obscured, allowing evil or danger to creep unnoticed. The "enemy" refers to the social, political, and possibly physical dangers lurking in the darkness, which the night aids .

question: The diction of Femi Osofisan's Morountodun can be described as
solution: The play addresses societal and political issues, with characters engaging in discussions that reveal the social, cultural and political struggles of the time. The dialogue in Morountodun uses rhetorical devices, persuasive language and impassioned speeches to convey its themes and persuade the audience to reflect on the issues at hand.

The powers would have failed if I did not let them use me. They knew my weakness: the weakness of a man easily moved to the defence of his tribe against others.'

O.Rotimi: The Gods are not to Blame.

'He says
The medicine gourds are filthy,
And the herb
Are drunk from unhygienic cups'

Okot p Bitek: Song of Lawino

The poet in the lines is saying that
solution:
In the lines, the speaker describes traditional herbal medicine as being kept in filthy gourds and drunk from unhygienic cups. This shows a strong disapproval of African traditional medicine, especially its methods and conditions of use. Although Western medicine is not mentioned directly, the poem Song of Lawino is built around a contrast between African traditions and Western ways. Whenever a character rejects or condemns a traditional practice in the poem, it implies an inclination toward Western practices, which are seen as cleaner and more modern.

The poet does not say that herbal medicine is dangerous; he only comments on its lack of hygiene. Therefore, option C goes beyond the text by introducing the idea of danger, which is not stated. Thus, the most appropriate interpretation of the speaker’s attitude in the lines is that he prefers Western medicine.

options.b: the person referred to takes Western medicine

solution:
A prose work that focuses on the moral growth and character formation of an individual is known as a psychological novel. This type of novel pays special attention to the inner thoughts, motivations, feelings and moral struggles of the protagonist.

A picaresque is a type of novel that follows the adventures of a roguish hero who moves from one episode to another. An autobiography is a self-written account of a person's life. While it may include moral growth, it is a genre of writing, not a specific type of novel centred on moral or psychological development. A satirical novel uses humour, irony and exaggeration to criticise society.

question: A literary work in which the characters and events are used as symbols is known as _________?

question: A poet‘s use of regular rhythm is known as _______?
solution:
In poetry, metre (Commonwealth English) or meter (American English) is the basic rhythmic structure of a verse or lines in verse. Many traditional verse forms prescribe a specific verse metre, or a certain set of metres alternating in a particular order. The study and the actual use of metres and forms of versification are both known as prosody. (Within linguistics, "prosody" is used in a more general sense that includes not only poetic metre but also the rhythmic aspects of prose, whether formal or informal, that vary from language to language, and sometimes between poetic traditions. Meter is a stressed and unstressed syllabic pattern in a verse, or within the lines of a poem. Stressed syllables tend to be longer, and unstressed shorter. In simple language, meter is a poetic device that serves as a linguistic sound pattern for the verses, as it gives poetry a rhythmical and melodious sound. For instance, if you read a poem aloud, and it produces regular sound patterns, then this poem would be a metered or measured poem. English poetry employs five basic meters, including:

  • Iambic meter (unstressed/stressed)

  • Trochaic meter (stressed/unstressed)

  • Spondaic meter, (stressed/stressed)

  • Anapestic meter (unstressed/unstressed/ stressed)

  • Dactylic meter (stressed/unstressed/unstressed)

  Meter has two subdivisions: qualitative meter, and quantitative meter.

  Qualitative Meter

  Qualitative meter contains stressed syllables with regular intervals, such as iambic pentameter containing even numbered syllables. Quantitative Meter Quantitative meter, however, is based on syllabic weight, and not stressed pattern,s such as dactylic hexameters of classical Greek and classical Latin. However, classical Arabic and Sanskrit also have used this meter. Poets like Virgil used quantitative meter in Aeneid, and Homer used it in Iliad.

  Short Examples of Meter; People become what they believe.

  (Trochaic meter)-Those who can dream it, they really can achieve it.

  (Dactylic/Spondaic)-Don’t search faults. Find remedies.

  (Iambic meter)-When you give and accept gratefully, you feel blessed.

 (Anapestic meter)-The safest place on planet earth.

question: A fable is a story in which________?
solution:
Fable is a literary device that can be defined as a concise and brief story intended to provide a moral lesson at the end. In literature, it is described as a didactic lesson given through some sort of animal story. In prose and verse, a fable is described through plants, animals, forces, of nature, and inanimate objects by giving them human attributes wherein they demonstrate a moral lesson at the end.

  Features of a Fable: fable is intended to provide a moral story. Fables often use animals as the main characters. They are presented with anthropomorphic characteristics, such as the ability to speak and to reason. Fables personify the animal characters.

options.b: animal is or things are used as characters
options.d: the story is told in poetic form

question:
The question is based on Richard Wright’s Native Son

Bigger kills Mary due to______?
solution: The protagonist and main character of Native Son, Bigger is the focus of the novel and the embodiment of its main theme—the effect of racism on the psychological state of its black victims. As a twenty-year-old black man cramped in a South Side apartment with his family, Bigger has lived a life defined by the fear and anger he feels toward whites for as long as he can remember. Bigger is limited by the fact that he has only completed the eighth grade, and by the racist real estate practices that force him to live in poverty. Furthermore, he is subjected to endless bombardment from a popular culture that portrays whites as sophisticated and blacks as either subservient or savage. Indeed, racism has severely curtailed Bigger’s prospects in life and even his very conception of himself. He is ashamed of his family’s poverty and afraid of the whites who control his life—feelings he works hard to keep hidden, even from himself. When these feelings overwhelm him, he reacts with violence. Bigger commits crimes with his friends—though only against other blacks, as the group is too frightened to rob a white man—but his own violence is often directed at these friends as well. Bigger feels little guilt after he accidentally kills Mary due to fear. But later on, he feels for the first time as though his life actually has meaning. Mary’s murder makes him believe that he has the power to assert himself against whites. Wright goes out of his way to emphasize that Bigger is not a conventional hero, as his brutality and capacity for violence are extremely disturbing, especially in graphic scenes such as the one in which he decapitates Mary’s corpse in order to stuff it into the furnace. Wright does not present Bigger as a hero to admire, but as a frightening and upsetting figure created by racism. Indeed, Wright’s point is that Bigger becomes a brutal killer precisely because the dominant white culture fears that he will become a brutal killer. By confirming whites’ fears, Bigger contributes to the cycle of racism in America. Only after he meets Max and learns to talk through his problems does Bigger begin to redeem himself, recognizing whites as individuals for the first time and realizing the extent to which he has been stunted by racism. Bigger’s progress is cut short, however, by his execution

question:
The question is based on Richard Wright’s NATIVE SON

Mr Dalton is of the opinion that Negroes are happier when they are
solution: Mr. Dalton has earned a fortune in real estate. Although he profits from charging high rents to poor black tenants—including Bigger’s family—on Chicago’s South Side, he nonetheless claims to be a generous philanthropist and supporter of black Americans. Mr. Dalton is a major player in the production of the “whiteness” that terrifies, oppresses, and enrages Bigger. Despite Bigger’s criminal record, Mr. Dalton gives him a job because he thinks that blacks deserve a chance. Nonetheless, there is condescension in Mr. Dalton’s manner and charity. He simultaneously profits from keeping blacks like Bigger’s family in terrible housing, and expresses alleged benevolence by giving Bigger a menial job.

question: The phrase “living death” in a literary work is an example_____________
solution: Oxymoron is a figure of speech in which two opposite ideas are joined to create an effect. The common oxymoron phrase is a combination of an adjective proceeded by a noun with contrasting meanings, such as “cruel kindness,” or “living death”. However, the contrasting words/phrases are not always glued together. The contrasting ideas may be spaced out in a sentence, such as, “In order to lead, you must walk behind.”

question: In drama, comic reliefs often occur in_______

question: 'A trouble shared is a trouble halved' illustrates the use of______

options.b: he loves Desdemona who is married to Othello
options.c: Othello is a better warrior that he is
options.d: Othello didn't make him the Lieutenant

question: The narrative technique adopted in Bayo Adebowale's Lonely Days is that of_________
solution: The story is told from the third person/omniscient point of view i.e the story is not told by any of the characters in the novel but by an outsider, who knows the storyline aforetime.

question: The specie of conflict exemplified in the Native Son is that of_______
solution: The main character, Wright's struggle as against the society's institutionalised problem of racism is predominant throughout the novel.

question: Amma Darko's Faceless may be best described as a___________
solution: The prose criticizes families and the entire society which directly or indirectly lure or lead their young ones into hard life on the streets. Baby T and Fofo's life stories are signal to these issues.

question: "You can call me a rebel, but I need a job. That's what matters to me." The repercussion of this statement by Aloho is a pointer to the theme ____
solution: If Aloho had listened to her best friend's warning against her working with Ochuole and Chief, her tragic end could have been avoided. Be reminded that the keyword in the question is "repercussion", otherwise, unemployment could have been the answer.

question: A short poem that expresses an idea in a clever way is called _____
solution: An Epigram is a short witty saying. Example, Knowledge is Power.

question: Adeoti's Ambush may be best described as____________
solution: The entire poem is metaphorical, as each line extensively conveys deeper meanings beyond the surficial expression and language. Although the direct comparisons in the poem maybe said to be analogical. However, this quality is better portrayed with Metaphor(s) as the writer did.

question: The most dominant poetic device used in Okara's 'Panio and Drums' is_______
solution: The essential messages in the poem are symbolical. The drums for example represent traditional African life, while the piano represents the Western world, this must be distinctively identified outside the use of metaphors.

question: In Lenrie's 'The Panic of Growing Older', another significant theme therein is on the___________
solution: The poem describes life in stages from childhood to adulthood alongside the experiences and uncertainties that follow at each phase.

question: The withdrawal of rest from man according to George Herbert in 'The Pulley' is particularly because_____
solution: In the third stanza of the poem, God gave his reason for withholding the gift of rest from man so that man can always remember his creator whether as a result of goodness or weariness.

options.a: man by nature has the tendencies to be lazy
options.b: of the need for human survival
options.c: God wants man to feel less of himself
options.d: of the space for God's worship

question: Blake's 'The School Boy' advocates for____________
solution: A fine balancing between work and pleasure for a child's development is the best answer. The poet in the sixth stanza concludes his argument by adding that strict parenting and schooling will make the child's adult life unproductive without a fine blend of enjoyment of life and discipline.

options.a: a fine balancing between work and pleasure for a child's development
options.b: the liberty of the child to explore nature and life without restraints
options.c: the promotion of the culture of excellence in a child's academic endavour
options.d: a proper disciplinary culture for the training of a child

question: A significant literary device employed by Morris in 'The Proud King' is that of________
solution: The poet portrays the importance of moral virtues and humility through the reversal of fortunes of the King.

question: Aloho begins her harvest of corruption when____________
solution: It is critical to note the figurative importance of Frank Ogodo's title, 'Harvest of Corruption', this literarily dawned on Aloho when she found out she was 3 months pregnant and the chances of abortion were impossible.

options.a: she found out the truth about Chief Ade Amaka
options.b: she discovered she was 3 months pregnant and abortion was impossible
options.c: her best friend warned her about the deceitfulness of Chief Ade Amaka
options.d: she eventually died after giving birth to a baby girl

question: The metaphorical significance of Frank Ogodo's 'Harvest of Corruption' is seen in the theme of_____________
solution: It must clearly pointed out that the repercussions of the activities of Chief Ade Amaka, Aloho, Madam Hoha, Justice Odili, Ochuole were properly served with punishment proportionately. This is the correlation of the title, 'Harvest of Corruption' with the theme of Retribution.

question: The voice of reason in the 'Harvest of Corruption' is exemplified in the character of___________
solution: Despite the depth of corruption and atrocities in the play, Ogeyi represents the voice of right reason and moral rectitude. She warned Aloho of the deceitfulness of Ochuole and sought justice for Aloho after her death.

question: The characterisation of Othello maybe pointed out in that__________
solution: The distinctive qualities of a character which the author defines or what the other characters perceive of him is characterization. Option C significantly points out this figure best in Othello.

options.a: he remains at a distance from much of the action that concerns and affects him at the beginning
options.b: he refuses to appoint Iago as the Lieutenant
options.c: although he is a cultural outsider in Venice, his military skills are valuable to the State
options.d: Iago sees him as an enemy

question: The setting of Shakespeare's Othello is____________
solution: Othello is set in Venice, Italy, at around the late half of the sixteenth century.

question: Bayo Adebowale portrayed Yaremi as resilient, self reliant and strong willed particularly to__________
solution: The author strongly condemned and advocates for the change in the African belief system as to male superiority over the female gender. Despite the resentment from most men in Kufi, Yaremi remained self-reliant and assertive. Option D although may seem tempting, but clearly relates to the unfounded beliefs that widows are responsible for their husbands' death etc.

options.b: represent the image of African traditionalism

question: 'They are as fearsome as they are fearless' is an example of______
solution: In an antithesis, two contradictory statements or words are placed far apart to create a juxtaposition.

question: In Herbert's "The Pulley", God's supremacy is exemplified through______
solution: God establishes his supremacy over man and retains man's dependency on Him by withholding the gift of rest so much so that man may continue to find strength and fulfilment by seeking and worshipping Him.

options.a: His withholding of the gift of rest
options.b: the insatiable nature of man's needs
options.d: His blessings of man with many gifts

question:
'The fair breeze blew
The white foam flew
The furrow followed free
We were the first that ever burst
Into that silent sea.'

The dominant figure of speech in the above passage is
solution:
Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds within a line of poem.

line 1 /b/ 'breeze' and 'blew'

line 2 /f/ 'foam' and 'flew'

line 3 /f/ ' furrow', 'followed' and 'free'

line 4 /w/ 'we' and 'were'

line 5 /s/ 'silent' and 'sea'

question: Awonoor's 'The Anvil and the Hammer' is best described as a/an___________
solution: The poem is a free verse, with no consistent metre pattern or rhythm.

question: The theme of the poem is__________
solution: The surrounding atmosphere of the poem and particularly lines 7 and 8 point respectively to the central theme of abomination which has overtaken the land and retributive justice i.e the vengance by the gods and spirits upon the land.

question: The mood of the poet is one of________
solution: The poet is apparently hopeless as he doesn't see any change or renewal in sight for his land. The loss of lives and abomination on the land are irredemable as well.

question: 'The earth has stopped breathing and sighed' illustrates__________
solution: The earth is directly given the attribute of a human being to create a metaphorical effect and meaning.

question: The run-on lines convey the speaker's_______
solution: The flow of words and expressions, from one to another, of the poet directly reflect his sorrowful state of mind for the tragedies that have consumed his land.

question: There is a predominance of words associated with________
solution: Throughout the poem, the reference to 'land', 'earth', 'harvest' 'rock' among related others point to the predominance of nature.

question: Which one of the following is most influential to the substance of Hallowell's "The Dinning Table"?
solution: The time and place including the prevailing occurence at the time when the poem was written, in Sierra Leone during the war, is of crucial importance as to defining why the poem bothers on brutality and the horrors of war.

question:
'Nightfall! Nightfall!
You are my mortal enemy'

The figurative name for the manner in which Nightfall is directly addressed is______
solution: Nightfall is addressed as though it were a living, breathing human being capable of response or action.

question: The mood of Diop's 'Vanity' is__________
solution: The mood of the poem is that of worry and the poet expressess this through a series of rhetorical questions.

options.c: expressed through the use of innuendos

question: ... is the illustration which serves to persuasively sustain the interest of the readers in a literary piece
solution: The narrative hook is that piece of writing which the writer used to draw and sustain the interest of his readers as the plot unfolds. This should not be mistakened with suspense which is meant to ignite the thinking and curiosity of the audiience.

question: In literary criticism, a casual reference to a figure or an event is regarded as a/an_______
solution: An allusion is a reference to an event, especially of similar quality or importance to establish or drive home a particular point or fact. An example here maybe seen in William Morris' "The Proud King"- biblical allusion.

question: A transferred epithet is popularly known as a/an ........
solution: Hypallage is the alternative or literary name for a transferred epithet. Example, a Bed of Thorns. Bed as used here is a transferred epithet or adjective as in this usage, for the qualification of the key word, 'Thorns'.

question: A dramatic type directed against an individual or a private institute with the intent to severely ridicule is called_________
solution: A satire is directed at the entire society while a lampoon is directy simply against an individual party.

question: A novel written in form of a letter is said to be_______
solution: An epistolary novel is that written as though it is a letter, reflective of the writer's personal feelings and indicating some form of close relationship interaction with the reader. 'So Long A Letter' by Mariama Ba is written in this format.

question: Adeoti's "Ambush" ends on a note of___________
solution: The last stanza of the poem, particularly the phrase 'awaiting in ambush' signifies hopelessness as the ruling class will be ready to devour the future change makers.

question: What incident ignited the excerpt above?
solution: Iago played upon Othello's insecurities, reminding him of Cassio's outstanding qualities, this accumulated to vexing Othello and luring him into thinking of Desdemona's betrayal.

options.a: the taking of a vow of revenge
options.c: Iago's conspiracy with Brabantio to kill Othello
options.d: Desdemona and Emilia's discussion on the lost handkerchief

question: The prevailing activity in the extract is
solution: Othello's rage having been ignited by Iago as it relates to Desdemona's betrayal made both parties kneel before each other to take a vow of revenge as against Desdemona.

options.a: the vow of revenge and pledge of loyalty by Iago to Othello
options.b: evident of the conspiracy between Iago and Othello
options.c: the vow by Iago to revenge upon Othello

question: Consequent upon the act in the excerpt, ...
solution: Consequent upon the joint oath swearing by Iago and Othello. Othello became a partner in evil with Iago, an incident which signifies the transformation of his characterisation from a hero to a villain.

options.a: Desdemona reconfessed her love for Othello
options.d: Othello changed from being the hero to a villain

question: The bar in Tennyson's "Crossing the Bar" is a symbol for the___________
solution: The bar apparently symbolises the boundary between life and death bearing in mind that the poet was ready to embark on a journey to the world of the unknown.

options.a: presence of God's and his guiding force

question: A story which explains a natural phenomenon is_______
solution: Myth is a traditional story that explains a natural phenomenon

question: ... may be said to be a critical subject of concern in Blake's "School Boy"
solution: Adventurism is the best answer. The poem is about a young boy's quest for freedom to explore life and nature outside strict parenting and academic endeavours.

question: Amma Darko's "Faceless" ended with________
solution: The novel ended by narrating how Baby T's spirit made Onko commit suicide while Maami Broni confesses all their deeds.

options.b: an epilogue narrating how Baby T's spirit revenged her death
options.c: an epilogue narrating how Fofo started her early childhood

question: Richard Wright's " Native Son" may be described to a/an_______
solution: Bildungsroman is a literary type which traces the lifestyle and personal developmental experiences of an individual across all phases or stages of life. The lifestyle of Thomas Bigger is the subject of target herein.

question: "The sudden throb of pain" in Lenrie's 'The Panic of Growing Old' signifies the_________
solution: In the poem, "The sudden throb of pain", "legs cribbed" signify weakness resulting from old age.

question: A character whose action or qualities serve to heighten those of the hero through contrast is__________
solution: The characterisation a foil is such that his or her role contradicts that of the main character. Ogeyi may be likened as a foil to Aloho in the Harvest of Corruption

question: A poem of 14 lines is known as__________
solution: Undoubtedly, a sonnet is a fourteen lines poem. An Octave, a verse of eight lines; Quatrain, with four lines; Sestet, with six lines.

question: Okara's reminiscence in the "Piano and Drums" as to how he walks to the farm with no shoes, marching thorns and dangerous leafs, and still finds this enjoyable creates a feeling of___________
solution: The poet ignites a nostlagic feeling- home coming, as he remembers and appreciates traditional and local green lifestyle which induces positive contemplation at the end.

options.b: neglect of African culture for civilized styles

question: Analogy is the arrangement of two ideas that are_________
solution: Similar is the best answer. As an example, "As the waters of the sea return to their pebbles, so our lives return to their graves".

question: ... a didactic poem, especially such with animals as characters may be distinctively described as a/an_________
solution: An Apologue is that type of moral fable with animals as characters. It usually conveys both surface and deeper meanings.

question: One of the following is not significant to Morris' "The Proud King"
solution: "The Proud King" is an epic poem which is didactic in nature- teaches moral lessons, its biblical allusion and concern for the sense of good virtue makes as well a parable of some kind.

question: 'The dun dun of a drum' is an example of an__________
solution: Onomatopoeia is a figurative expression which uses words that are suggestive of sounds to convey certain meanings. E.g. 'Tick Tock' says the clock.

question:
"A time to sow,
A time to reap,
A time to born,
A time to die"

This exemplies the use of ...
solution: Anaphora is the repitition of a word or phrase at the beginning of each successive lines of poetry.

question: Asides being a satire, Ogodo's "Harvest of Corruption" may also be regarded as a________
solution: The play is also a tragedy and it demonstrates how Aloho endures the years of joblessness, the scenario of her arrest for drug trafficking and eventually her death.

question: Chief Ade Amaka portrays_______
solution: Through his public office as Minister of External Relations, Chief Ade Amaka portrays the betrayal of public trust reposed in his office by embezzling public funds and engaging in other criminal activities.

question: 'poisonous pleasure of wine' illustrates the use of_______
solution: Oxymoron places two contradictory words side by side. The placement of 'poisonous' and 'pleasant' side by side is reflective of this.

question: Fatalism is crucial element in_________
solution: Fatalism is the ideology that everything in life is predetermined by supernatural causes. In tragedy, this ideology is prevalent as the protagonist's downfall is inevitable and is seen as more or less his/her destiny.

question:
The question is based on George Orwell's NINETEEN EIGHTY-FOUR

The novel draws a picture of______?

The question is based on George Orwell's NINETEEN EIGHTY-FOUR.

The power and oppression of an irresistible evil debased Winston’s dreams of________?

The question is based on George Orwell's NINETEEN EIGHTY-FOUR.

The novel can be described as__________?

The question is based on George Orwell's NINETEEN EIGHTY-FOUR.

Room 101 symbolises a place of________???

The question is based on J.P Clark's WIVES' REVOLT.

In the play the central idea is that gender equity_______?
solution: Clark uses the text to agitate for gender equity. He is of the opinion that gender equity is desirable and attainable through a peaceful protest.

question:
The question is based on J.P Clark's WIVES' REVOLT.

In their fight, the women settle at Eyara in order to_______?
solution: There are only three characters in this drama, making the story easy to follow from the outset. It clearly dramatises the injustice the women suffer at the hands of their men. Unlike in traditional times, when the fairer sex had to swallow whatever males gave them, the wives (and even the mothers of the men) of the Erhuwaren don’t take ‘no’ for an answer. They know how to fight for their rights. Okoro represents the deepest kinds of misogyny: when men don’t help their women to do the chores, don’t give their wives enough money for food and yet complain the soup isn’t tasty. Or those who beat up their partners in a drunken state and cap it all by accusing them of crimes they haven’t committed (Chinelo Oputa). In their flight the women settle at iyara in order hurt their husbands

question:
The question is based on J.P Clark's WIVES REVOLT.

"Those who have full breasts have walked out, and that leaves you, me, and the old girls returned_______

options.a: front of the veranda of Okoro's house
options.d: the direction of the kitchen, off stage

question:
This question is based on George Orwell's 1984.

'Big Brothers Is Watching You' Big Brother Controls life in Oceania Though the four ministries of peace, Love, plenty, are Truth.
The Couple continue to meet secretly in an attic room above a junk shop owned by
solution: Mr. Charrington, was the man who sold the diary, and later a coral paperweight to Winston.

question:
This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's The Joys of Motherhood.

Nnaife and Nnu Ego Second Child but the first to live is
solution: In the novel, the death of the first child "Ngozi led to Nnu Ego's committing suicide at the waterfront to Cater Bridge. The second child is therefore called Oshia, who belongs to Nnaife.

question:
This question is based on William Shakespeare’s Romeo and Juliet.

Romeo is evicted to__________because he__________
solution: Romeo was expelled or banished to Mantua for killing Tybalt in a street combat.

options.a: Mantua, fights Tybalt in a street duel
options.b: Capulet's party, kills Tybalt in a street duel
options.c: Mantua, fights and kills Tybalt in a street duel
options.d: Mantua, kills Paris in a street duel.

question:
This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's The Joys of Motherhood.

The relationships of Nnaife family are
solution: In the novel, Nnaife lost his job. Nnu Ego struggled to feed the family. Nnaife's family was sent out of their previous apartment; Nnu Ego rented a small apartment and finally travelled to the village of Ogboli in Ibuza homeland where she ended her life.

question:
This question is based on J.C. De Graft's Sons and Daughters

A brother to Aaron and Maanan is
solution: This is found on the third page of the book, where all characters and their roles in the play are written.

question:

Where does the play take place?
solution: The play took place in Ofosu's place because James Ofosu had allowed himself to be manipulated by Lawyer Bonu and seems to follow Lawyer Bonu's advice blindly.

question:
This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's The Joys of Motherhood.

Why did Nnaife blame Nnu Ego for all his problems
solution: I Kehinde, the daughter of Nnaife broke his father's (Nnaife) rules by running away with a Yoruba man, he assaulted the father of Kehinde's husband. Sent to prison, Nnaife therefore blamed Nnu Ego for all his problems.

options.b: Because his daughter disobeyed him by travelling to abroad
options.c: Because his daughter disobeyed him by moving with prostitutes
options.d: Because his daughter ran away with a Yoruba man.

Paternal aunt to Aaron and Maanan is
solution: This can easily be found at the beginning of the play where all the characters are listed.

question:

In Romeo and Juliet, the major role of Mercutio is to
solution: Mercutio and Benvolio who were Romeo's friends were waiting on the street when Tybalt arrives with the intention of challenging/punishing Romeo for sneaking into the party. Mercutio aggressively jumps into Romeo and Tybalt issues and drew out his sword on Tybalt but Tybalt cuts Mercutio.

options.a: Count Paris in Praise of Juliet
options.d: serve as an assistance to Romeo

In Nnaife's house, life became miserable to Nnu Ego by the news that

options.b: Nnaife has inherited buildings and properties
options.d: Nnaife has inherited his brother's wives and children.

question:
This question is based on Buchi Emecheta's The Joys of Motherhood.

In the novel, it is necessary that Ona has to leave her father's house because
solution: It is obvious that once Ona is married, she must leave her father's house except she gives birth to a boy. Unfortunately, she dies during childbirth.

options.a: of the safety of her child

solution:
In Purple Hibiscus, Father Benedict represents a rigid, conservative Catholic tradition. At St. Agnes’ Church, he insists on strict adherence to traditional (colonial-influenced) Catholic practices. One of the changes highlighted in the novel is that the Kyrie (“Lord, have mercy”) must be said only in Latin, not in Igbo. This reflects the broader theme in the novel of:

  • Religious rigidity

  • Suppression of indigenous culture

  • Preference for Western forms of worship over local expression

The device employed in the line above from Kunene's The Heritage of Liberation, is an example of
solution:
Apostrophe is a literary device in which a speaker directly addresses an absent person, a dead person, an inanimate object, or an abstract idea as if it were present and capable of responding.

In the line "Since it was you who in all these thin seasons" from Kunene's The Heritage of Liberation, the speaker appears to be addressing someone absent or not directly present, making it an example of apostrophe.

The figure of speech in the line above from Acquah's In the Navel of the Soul is
solution: Assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds within words. In the line: "We would be believing we dreamt it all", The /i:/ sound appears in "we", "be" and "believing"; and the /i/ sound in  "believing" and "it". This repetition of vowel sounds is a clear example of assonance.

A play in which the acts succeed one another without probable or necessary sequence is
solution: An episodic play is characterised by a structure where the acts or scenes follow one another without a strict logical or necessary sequence. This type of play focuses on individual episodes or events, which may be loosely connected but do not rely on a continuous or linear progression. It allows for more flexibility in storytelling and emphasises themes or character development over plot continuity.

Criticism is a literary activity which seeks to
solution: Criticism in the context of literature involves the examination and assessment of literary works. It goes beyond simply finding faults; it includes analyzing the themes, characters, writing style, and other elements to provide an evaluation or interpretation of the work. Critics may explore the strengths and weaknesses of a piece, offer insights into its meaning, and contribute to a deeper understanding of literature.

When two statements or comparisons are apparently contradictory, we have an example of
solution:
A paradox is a figure of speech or rhetorical device in which two seemingly contradictory statements or ideas are presented together, but on closer inspection, they reveal a deeper truth or make a powerful point.

Example:

  • "The more you know, the more you realize you don't know."

  • "Freedom is slavery." — from George Orwell's 1984

These examples appear contradictory at first but contain an underlying truth upon reflection.

This question is based on General Literary Principles.

'If Winter comes, can Spring be far behind?' Shelly, 'Ode to the West Wind'. The literary device used here is
solution:
The literary device used in the line “If Winter comes, can Spring be far behind?” is rhetoric.

 Rhetoric is a literary device that uses language to persuade or influence an audience. In this case, the line is a rhetorical question that is meant to inspire hope and optimism. The question is not meant to be answered literally, but rather to encourage the reader to think positively about the future.

solution: A tragic flaw (also called hamartia) is a fundamental weakness or error in judgment in the protagonist that leads to their downfall in a tragedy. This flaw is tied to an excess of a particular trait, such as ambition, pride, jealousy or indecisiveness.

'With a running stream and a water-mill beating the darkness. And three trees on the low sky.'
In the excerpt above from Eliot's Journey of the Magi, the dominant literary device is
solution: Personification is a literary device in which human qualities are given to non-human objects or elements of nature. In the excerpt:"With a running stream and a water-mill beating the darkness. And three trees on the low sky." The phrase "a water-mill beating the darkness" gives human-like action (beating) to the water-mill, which is an inanimate object. This is a clear example of personification.

solution: The passage describes a poor harvest where "many farmers wept" and one man even "hanged himself." These details create a gloomy, sorrowful, and depressing atmosphere, which conveys a strong sense of sadness.

question: A Literary work that ridicules the shortcomings of people or ideas is ......
solution: A satire ridicles people or ideas; points out their wrong in order for them to change for the better.

question: The exclusive right given to authors to protect their works from unlawful production is
solution: A copyright prevents other people from producing an intellectual work without the permission of the author.

question: Empathy is achieved when the audience
solution: Empathy occurs when the audience shares in the stage experience. The audience in the theatre follows performance and simultaneously understands the feelings of the dramatis personae.

options.b: vicariously participates in the stage experience

question: Mock-heroic poetry elevates
solution: The mock-epic genre of poetry is one that uses the elevated and serious style of the epic to discuss trivial and unserious issues.

options.c: the stripping off of appearances in a witty manner

solution: This is the major message in a literary work.

question: A dramatic type directed against an individual or a private institute with the intent to severely ridicule is called
solution: A lampoon is a type of dramatic work, often in the form of writing, that aims to severely ridicule or mock an individual, group, or institution. It can be humorous, but its intent is to criticize or satirize in a harsh manner.

question:

solution: Anaphora is the repitition of a word or phrase at the beginning of each successive lines of poetry.

question: An irredeemable reversal of the hero's fortune in a tragedy is called.
solution: Peripeteia is the reversal of fortune suffered by a tragic hero. It is believed that when a tragic character makes a mistake, such mistake must be punished. Therefore, the consequences of such becomes irredeemable.

question: "I hate brave cowards like you" is an example of
solution: The reason is that there is the placement of two contradicting words placed side by side.

question: The narrator in a prose work who is also a character is
solution: A participatory narrator is a narrator who is also a character in the story. This type of narrator shares experiences, thoughts and actions from their own perspective, while also being involved in the events of the narrative. The story is told from the inside, as the narrator participates directly in the events being described.

question:
'The fair breeze blew
The white foam flew
The furrow followed free
We were the first that ever burst into that silent sea.'

The dominant figure of speech in the above passage is
solution: Alliteration is the repetition of consonant sounds at the beginning of words in close proximity. In the passage, the repetition of the "f" sound in "foam flew," and "furrow followed free", the repetition of the "b" sound in "breeze blew" and the repetition of the "s" sound in "silent sea"  exemplifies alliteration. This technique creates a rhythmic and musical quality to the passage.

question: Which of these is the oldest genre of literature?
solution: Poetry is the oldest form of literature.

question: A device used by a writer to recall past event in a literary work is
solution: Flash back. It brings events from the past to the moment.

question: That quality in a literal work which evokes tenderness, pity or sorrow is

question: Black theatre is a drama concerned with
solution: Black theatre is a form of drama that is primarily concerned with exploring and depicting the identity, experiences, and cultural heritage of Black Americans.

question: A final stanza of a poem that is shorter than the preceding one is called
solution: It is a short final verse of a poem or stanza, that is used to address or make a comment.

question: Monometer is a
solution:
A monometer is a mine of verse consisting of one metrical foot. This is the poetic metre that consists of a single metrical foot per line. This means that each line of verse has only one stressed and one unstressed syllable. e.g.  

Freedom.

options.a: Line of verse consisting of a metrical foot
options.b: Single foot used in John Milton's sonnets
options.c: line repeated in first syllable of a word

question: A character that heavily relies on cultural types for his manner of speech, his personality and other distinguishing characteristics is
solution: A stock character is a frictional character, based on common literary or social stereotypes. The characters are often recognizable by certain traits or behaviour associated with their role which are often found in the social environment of the readers.

question: The form of poetry that celebrates with nostalgia the ideal world of the countryside is referred to as
solution: A pastoral is concerned with simple peaceful country life. This is all about worldview in the agrarian English Christian villages, before the industrial revolution.

question:
'To see a world in a grain of sand
And a heaven in a wild flower
Hold infinity in the palm of your hand
And eternity in an hour'
- William Blake
To see a World In a Grain of Sand.
The predominant figure of speech used in the lines above is
solution: A paradox is a figure of speech that presents contrasting statements that may be confusing at first but upon closer examination begins to make sense. I the idea of seeing the world in a grain of sand, and holding infinity in the palm of your hands is paradoxical, and emphasises the vastness found in small and seemingly insignificant things.

question:
I am informed the day recycles itself today
When I slept in the Lord in February .
I blame no one for an untimely death
I bless God for a noble departure- His words The Lord taketh the righteous away before evil days.'

The persona in the poem appear happy to have died
solution: He blesses the Lord for taking him before evil days, implying he was still admired and not disgraced before his death – a noble death.

question: The tragic character is the person whose experiences arouse pity and
solution: The experience of the tragic hero arouses pity and fear.

question: A panegyric poem is composed to
solution: The panegyric poem is composed to offer high praisre to a person or thing. It is also known as a eulogy.

question: A trilogy is the
solution: Trilogy is sequence of three plays by the same author in different parts.

options.a: set of three one-act drama written by related authors
options.b: sequence of three plays written by the same author
options.c: series of related stories divided into three equal parts
options.d: collection of three poems of equal length

question: The term given to a type of incident or device which recurs frequently in literature is
solution: Motif is the repetition of a literary theme in different works of literature.

question: The term for the moral flaw or weakness that leads to downfall of a major character in drama is
solution:
The moral weakness leading to the death of hero is known as hamartia.
This is also know as *tragic flaw*. If this moral weakness is in the form of pride, it i referred to as hubris.

question: The narrator in a prose work who is also a character is
solution: A participatory narrator is a character within the prose work who not only tells the story but is also directly involved in the events of the narrative. This type of narrator provides a first-person perspective, as they are a participant in the story they are recounting. The participatory narrator can offer insights, thoughts, and emotions based on their personal experiences and involvement in the plot.

question:
Africa my Africa of proud warriors in ancestral savannahs.
I have never known you
but your blood flows in my vein
Your beautiful black blood that irrigates
the field...
David Diop, 'Africa'
In the lines above, Diop uses
solution: A drop expresses Africa as a continent as if it has human attributes.

question: The figure of speech in which a character makes a statement that has or would have deep and serious implication in the play is
solution:
The question describes a situation where a character makes a statement that seems ordinary to them but has deep or hidden implications in the context of the play, implications that the audience understands, even if the character does not.

This is exactly what dramatic irony is:

  • The audience knows more than the character.

  • A character’s words carry a meaning or consequence unknown to them but clear to the audience.

solution: A paradox is a figure of speech that uses complex, contradictory statements in a way that seems confusing at first but upon closer examination, has deeper meaning. The theme of the poem "Give Me The Minstrel's Seat" centres on
solution: This traditional Swahili poem from Kenya centres on companionship. As social beings, no one  is expected to live a life of solitude. It salks about companionship in basic aspects of life such as marriage and friendship. He talks about how a woman is incomplete without a partnership. It is this partnership that leads discord among their enemies. He also talks about how we should not walk alone. He says "he who walks alone has no good fortune".

The statement above refers to
solution: The statement refers to Romeo. Juliet calls him a serpent because she was surprised He could kill.

question:
This question is based on William Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet

The spatial setting of the play is
solution: The spatial setting of the play is in Verona which is in Venice, Italy.

question:
This question is based on William Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet

Romeo is banished from Mantua because he
solution: Romeo is banished from Mantua because he killed Tybalt in a street duel.

options.a: kills Tybalt in a street's duel
options.d: attempts to kill Paris, his rival

question:
This question is based on J.C. De Graft's Sons and Daughters.

Maanan expresses dislike for lawyer B because of
solution: Maanan expresses dislike for lawyer B because of his recent advances towards her. Manaan finds this very annoying and immoral because she is from a decent family and this man is her father's best friend and he is also married.

options.a: the betrayal of his wife's trust
options.b: the betrayal of her father's trust
options.c: his condemnation of her choice of career

question:
This question is based on J.C. De Graft's Sons and Daughters.
